title: Constant Prop to Avoid False Index Out of Bounds
body:
Some downstream tools will emit warnings or errors if a vector can be indexed out of bounds. However, not all tools are smart enough to know when something is actually a constant and can't be out of bounds.

Consider the following two Verilog circuits. Both contain a single-element vector indexed by a 1-bit wire. However, `Foo` cannot cause an out-of-bounds error (unless you zap it with some Verilog `force` thing). `Bar` can result in an out-of-bounds error if `addr` is `1'b1`.

```verilog
module Foo(
  output rData);

  reg  memory[0:0];     
  wire memory_r_addr;   

  assign memory_r_addr = 1'h0;  
  assign rData = memory[memory_r_addr]; /* False index out-of-bounds */
endmodule
```

```verilog
module Bar(
  input  addr,
  output rData);

  reg memory[0:0];	

  assign rData = memory[addr]; /* Actual index out-of-bounds (from what is visible here...) */
endmodule
```

Emitting things like `Foo` is problematic because if I'm running a specific downstream tool, I may need to globally disable index out-of-bounds warnings/errors in order to get it to work. (I have a tool here that won't accept `Foo` unless I turn off out-of-bounds index warnings.) However, I'm then missing things like `Bar`. 

There are a couple of ways to handle this:

1) It would be good if this was constant propagated or copy propagated (I'm a broken record...). It generally seems like a good idea to move constants into indexes if possible. Note that this is weird because this is an optimization required for "correctness". See discussion below.
2) We could add per-tool options to emit the code that specific tools want or add Verilog attributes/comments that disable specific checks on specific lines. E.g., `--vendor=<vendor> --tool=<tool>`. This is going to get very tedious and a massive amount of work until we take over the world and EDA vendors start hiring people to improve our output to be compatible with their tools. Likely this is a punt for now.
3) We don't handle it and we start some documentation page/rationale doc to explain quirks of different tools and why we do/don't emit certain things a certain way. This may also include canned snippets for how to run things. This may bump up against legal problems if it exposes vendor tool APIs.

### Discussion

#### Constant Propagation Required for "Correctness"?

It's weird here that in order for our Verilog to be "correct" (by some definition of a downstream tool) we need to run an _optimization_ pass. (@youngar has brought up this exact point before and may have some thoughts.) I generally don't like this, but there may be some analogies from software compilers that we can use here? 

Or: hardware and the hardware ecosystem is a patchwork of different tools accepting different things. We turn on optimizations that avoid things like this by default. Deviating from specific optimizations being on may cause problems with tools.

#### Scope of Analysis

In order for this to really be "correct" we need global constant propagation. E.g., the `Bar` circuit could be totally safe if it's instantiator is passing a `1'b0` into its `addr` port. This is problematic/annoying because: (1) it's a global analysis and (2) it turns a port into dead code which may be undesirable for an end user. E.g., some verification engineer may expect every instantiation of `Bar` to have an `addr` port. If we remove it or stop using it internal to `Bar` this may cause problems.

### Source MLIR

```mlir
module attributes {firrtl.mainModule = "Foo"}  {
  rtl.module @Foo() -> (%rData: i1) {
    %false = rtl.constant false
    %memory = sv.reg  : !rtl.inout<uarray<1xi1>>
    %memory_r_addr = sv.wire  : !rtl.inout<i1>
    %0 = sv.read_inout %memory_r_addr : !rtl.inout<i1>
    sv.connect %memory_r_addr, %false : i1
    %1 = sv.array_index_inout %memory[%0] : !rtl.inout<uarray<1xi1>>, i1
    %2 = sv.read_inout %1 : !rtl.inout<i1>
    rtl.output %2 : i1
  }
}

module attributes {firrtl.mainModule = "Bar"}  {
  rtl.module @Bar(%addr: i1) -> (%rData: i1) {
    %memory = sv.reg  : !rtl.inout<uarray<1xi1>>
    %0 = sv.array_index_inout %memory[%addr] : !rtl.inout<uarray<1xi1>>, i1
    %1 = sv.read_inout %0 : !rtl.inout<i1>
    rtl.output %1 : i1
  }
}
```
